# Transcrição: 03-The Punisher Method to Reset Performance 

- **Fonte:** 03-The Punisher Method to Reset Performance 
- **Duração:** 08:10
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

to introduce you to something I call the Punisher method. And it's something which I used to use a lot more before CBO, Campaign Budget Optimization became a thing. So the idea behind the Punisher method is that there are certain signals that you send Facebook with the budget decisions you make. So here's the thing. If you increase your budget, 10%, 100%, 1000%, whatever, you're telling the algorithm that you're happy with results. Now, whether you are or not, you know, that really depends on your actions. Why would you increase the budget so much unless you're speculating or you're satisfied with results? So that goes back to Facebook as a positive signal. However, one thing that I found many years ago is that the opposite is also true. So if you decrease your budget, Facebook sees that as a negative signal, and often it kicks starts the performance. So I'll give you an example. If you go through the graduation testing process, and you've landed your ad sets, your campaigns are doing well, and you know, three, four, five days down the line, campaign results start to fluctuate. You are ready to increase your spend, but you haven't done yet. You steady it, you optimize it, you're getting good performance, and then you start bumping your budgets up 20% increase on day one, 20% day two, 20% on day three, and performance is looking good. And you're really excited, you're really happy. But on the third, fourth, fifth day, performance starts to fluctuate, and then it then it starts to suffer. And there's no reason to, you know, you've got great conversion rate previously, and all the stats have been performing well. But now for some reason, and you've gone through the analysis and you can't find what's gone wrong, you're just not able to maintain the performance you had. One of the strategies you can use at that point is to reduce your budget. And what I would do is reduce it by 10%. That often kick starts the algorithm to find better users. Because what I believe happens at that point is, when you reduce your budgets, and it's and it's and it's by an arbitrary amount like 10%, if you spend $100 day 1000 $10,000 day, in the context of how much you're spending in that campaign, 10% isn't a huge amount. But I found that that is enough to tell the algorithm that you're not happy with performance. And it's almost like it short circuits the performance of your ad sets, and kicks them into shape. So often what it will do is when I and I use this more, more, most commonly when I'm scaling. So when I'm doing, whether it's a moderate scaling or more aggressive, if I'm in the scaling process, so you know, in the scaling section, we'll cover it off in more detail. But if I'm doing whether it's 20% every day, 20% every other day, or maybe bumps within the day 50% 100%. If I feel like the

## [03:04 - 06:05]

algorithm has taken my additional budget and either wasted it or attempted to push it into audiences, just that just not converting, I'll pull that budget back, reduce it by 10%. And give it at least 24 hours, if not 48 hours to recover performance and generally it works. Now that CBO is the way that I manage my campaigns and my budgets, it still works at the CBO level. And what I want to show you here is showing you how CBO actually makes a lot of those decisions for you. So going back before CBO and when I used to only do ad set budgets, I would have to do this at the ad set level almost every day, especially when I was scaling. But now I find at the ad set level that Facebook is doing this for me, Facebook is running the Punisher method, just like I used to manually. And I don't need to do it so much at the ad set level. But there are times where I might need to step in and control and optimize my performance. So I've selected this one campaign here just to give you an example of how this looks. It's launched three weeks ago. And you can see the performance here. Our benchmark ROAS target is anything above two. It's really good. Anything above 1.5 is profitable. So currently in this three week period, performance is good. And you can see overall, you know, we've had a bit of bouncing. And, you know, we haven't really, you know, we've kind of plateaued at that level. Budgets have increased. So 13, 12, and then 1500. But fundamentally, the performance has been there. Now if I break that down by ad set, and we look a bit deeper as to what's going on, there's three ad sets running at the moment. And you can see the ad set performance for ad set one is 1.6 and then 2.5 and 2.25. What Facebook has been doing, and we haven't optimized or controlled where Facebook is putting the spend is Facebook is still speculating on where the performance best is coming from. So here, for example, the 1.61, it's still above our kind of break even and minimum target. The 2.5 is doing extremely well. 2.25 is kind of in between. And on average, it's pulling down our ROAS. So for example, if that $1,000 will distribute to these two ad sets, you'd look at that and say, you know, that would give me far better performance. But what Facebook has been doing is speculating on the spend and balancing its opportunities. So you know, if you go, this is week one at the bottom, 9th of April to 15th. And Facebook's spend levels have been very dramatically different. And each week, those spends are fluctuating based on where Facebook sees the opportunities and performance. Now in this particular week here, I would argue that it didn't spend the budget in the best way. So $484 went into here, which is much higher than the budget that went into this ad set here. And

## [06:05 - 08:10]

you look at the ROAS, they're quite different. So, you know, on that kind of balance, you'd say that the budget hasn't best been invested by Facebook in terms of these ad sets. So the question is, what do you do about that? Now previously, when the budgets, when I would set budgets at the ad set level, I would reduce the budget of this ad set down, maybe by 10% and bump these budgets up to give Facebook the signals that I'm not happy with performance. So the question is, with CBO, how do you do that? How do you give the signals that you're not happy with performance at the ad set level? So one thing I would do is to actually drill down to the ad level and see what's happening with my ads that are operating. Now, if I'm seeing that certain ads are taking too much spend and they're not performing, then I'll pull them out the auction and I'll try and optimize the spend that way. So my first port of call is to pull ads out. This for me has the minimal impact on the auction and performance. So one thing here is to identify your low performers and pause them out so that the spend will go to the ad sets and the ads are performing, especially if ultimately all of the ads are not performing, I'll pause all the ads out. The other thing that you can do, which I don't do as often, is that you can set a max spend limit for the ad set that's not performing and you can force the spend to go into your other ad sets. So the punishment method is more about kind of controlling budget at the high level and then the second method that I use alongside this is called ad sniper. An ad sniper is something which I'll explain in a separate video, but it's about snipping, pruning, whatever word you want to use, but taking out those bad performers and then letting Facebook spend where your profitability actually is.
